Confronting Line Cutters.
body:
I was in a Ratnadeep store buying some groceries. I went to the counter to pay as usual. There were two people in front of me, an elderly man and a woman in her 30s. As the woman was paying, the elderly man cut the line to get something he forgot. While he was gone, the woman finished her transaction and left. It took another 20 seconds for him to return. I stood there along with another woman behind me out of courtesy for the old man.  When he returned, seeing us waiting, he apologized and thanked us. We greeted him and smiled back. Fair enough

Now it was my turn, as I was reaching to my pocket, 'backassward-ly" a young man in his teens enters the shop, grabs himself a loaf of bread and stands right in front of me. I froze for a second. It was the most extraordinary transition to an "unexplored territory". The cashier herself was visibly irritated. She told him that she was attending to me and that he should fall back in line just like everyone else. He smirked and stood behind; in between the lady and me. I couldn't handle his behavior and flipped. I said to his face, what he was doing was wrong and he should have the least courtesy towards other customers. He had that look in the face which completely describes and ghetto teen filled with cynicism and packed with attitude. 

The woman behind me told me to calm down and let him get over with. I paid for my shit and left.

As I reflect now, there is one thing I realize. If we wish to change the country for the better, it should start from us. At the end of the day, you can only change yourself. As citizens, we must start taking responsibilities and not be those who vocally pledge nationalism, but those who live by the oath in action.

In light of the impending probable implementation of NRC in West Bengal, I feel it is my duty to tell the story of my family (and in part, of my community) before it is lost in a sea of thousand stories.
body:
I did not wanted to get so personal on the Internet but I have to do this.

I am a lower caste hindu bengali also known as Namashudra. Although I call myself an agnostic hindu, people generally don’t see it that way. I don’t use my SC certificate for any favours which in turn angers everyone in my family. My grandfather came to India in 1955 from east Pakistan. He came with a wife and two sons (my father was one of them). My uncle and aunt were born later in India. You could say that they were the first Indians of my family. My grandfather settled in a small town by the river, on illegally acquired marshland, like many others who came and settled here. It took my family 40 years to come out of poverty. I remember that very clearly as it happened during my childhood. My generation was the first in my family to even finish school and go to college.

Namashudras have been suffering for hundreds of years under the British and also hindu zamindars and muslim nawabs but the partition hit us the most hard. Before he had to come here, my grandfather was an independent fisherman in his own land but here he was a contract labour living on borrowed land. India welcomed the refugees but the people never really opened their hearts to us. I don’t want to generalise but this prejudice and alienation persists still today. For some elite ‘bhumiputra’ hindu bengalis, the Namashudra refugees rank lower than ‘bhumiputra’ muslim bengalis, no matter the circumstance. I can forgive but how can I forget the taunts from some of my friends that I have to endure despite being an Indian just like them. They do not feel like innocent japes.

I am politically a bit left leaning but I don’t trust any political parties. In the last 70 years all parties have exploited our community for votes and I reckon they will continue to do so. My own uncle (who was the first Indian in my family) was arrested forcefully and killed in police custody (sometime between 1972-1974) because he had naxal friends and thus considered dangerous. All my life, people have tried to ignite my supposed resentment towards my country and they have failed. I have and always will love India and its people. Despite its flaws, I still have faith in the system. I do not have any anger towards any country, religion or community or hate all the people because of crimes committed by some of them who are lost. I still trust humanity.

So what has all this got to do with NRC? It is a complicated process to say the least and conducted by the Indian bureaucracy. Many mistakes will be made, both honest and dishonest. My family and many like us has some of the needed documents but they are in a sorry condition. It all boils down to the availability of our legacy data kept by the government. Even after all that, me or any of my relatives or people in my community whom I know personally, who have lived in India for many decades might lose their citizenship due to a technical glitch or clerical error. Many might get citizenship illegally. But this is not the time to panic or start fear-mongering but to become alert to our current predicament as a law abiding citizen of India. I do not claim to know all the technicalities but I have started to inform people in my locality as best as I can, to prepare the documents and get ready for this process as civililised people.

But some questions remain. Why only border states? Why not the whole country? Why not every region with or without any special allocation for any special community? Why only certain religious immigrants are invalid? If the law is the law and law is equal for everyone then why not all illegal immigrants are shown the door? India has enough mouths to feed. Does it need more come flooding in just because they are minority in another country but majority here? I am willing to take that risk but what about you?

I apologise in advance for not being able to reply to your comments on time.

Jai Hind.

Kashmiri student based in Bangalore files petition before the Karnataka HC against communication shutdown
body:
​

https://i.redd.it/qkfwmyh0jik31.png

**September 04, 2019:** It has almost been one month since the Central Government imposed a complete communication shutdown in Kashmir on 5 August 2019. While restrictions have been eased in the Jammu region, the Kashmir valley continues to remain under clamp down with internet and cellular network services suspended. On 19 August 2019, a young Kashmiri student based in Bangalore moved to the Karnataka High Court to challenge the complete communication blockade as violative of fundamental rights of residents of Kashmir and their loved ones living outside the State.   


The Petitioner was forced to approach the High Court because he had been unable to establish contact with his family members in Kashmir. He was especially worried because many of his family members suffer from various health problems and due to the communication shutdown and strict curfews, it has become difficult to call for help or reach hospitals in time. The Petition argues that the complete communication blockade violates Articles 14, 19(1)(a) and 21 of the Constitution of India and it is incompatible with India’s obligations under international human rights law. Most importantly, the Petition highlights that the communication shutdown seems to lack any legal basis because there is no order authorizing the suspension of telecom and internet services which is available in the public domain.   


As a constitutional democracy, what sets India apart is our stated commitment to the rule of law. While imposing such severe and indiscriminate restrictions on an entire community, the Government must at the very least act in a transparent manner. Without access to the legal orders, it is impossible to know under which law the shutdown has been ordered, on what grounds, by which government authority, for what period of time, and whether it has been subjected to any kind of review.  


There have been three hearings in the case so far on 27 August, 30 August and 3 September. The crux of these hearings has been the maintainability of the Petition in light of a similar petition filed by Mr. Tehseen Poonawalla being pending before the Supreme Court. During the hearings, the counsel for the Petitioner has clarified that this case is different because the Petitioner is personally affected by the communication shutdown. Further, High Courts in India have independent and concurrent powers to protect fundamental rights of individuals within their territorial jurisdiction. To us, this is also an access to justice issue because an aggrieved person cannot be compelled to travel all the way to the Supreme Court in Delhi to seek constitutional remedies.  


Internet Freedom Foundation along with Ms. Maansi Verma assisted in drafting the Writ Petition and provided inputs on strategy and policy. The Petition was filed and argued by Mr. Vishwajith Sadanand and Mr. Ravi Varma Kumar and we deeply appreciate their support. While at the moment, IFF does not have the capacity to be a full fledged legal clinic, we do take on this role whenever there is a serious violation of rights of internet users in any part of India. This Petition is part of our larger advocacy against internet shutdowns which are a pan-India problem. Internet shutdowns cause great hardships because ordinary people cannot access many government benefits and essential services such as banking without the internet. In today’s digitized world, the internet cannot be considered a luxury, and access to the internet is a fundamental right and any restrictions on it must be subjected to constitutional scrutiny.   


**Important Links:**

1. Copy of Writ Petition ([**link**](https://drive.google.com/viewerng/viewer?url=https://www.livelaw.in/pdf_upload/pdf_upload-363995.pdf))

What's​ up with the way we drive?
body:
So this happened a while ago. I work in Mumbai and had bought a car recently. I drive to work everyday and the more I drive, I notice how less we care about rules when driving. This is a funny post though so read ahead

This one time I was stuck in traffic and It was bad. To the point were there was barely a centimeter of space between my cars side mirror and the next cars side mirror. A biker pulls up to my car window and stops because there's no place to get by. He knocks on my window and I roll it down. He then proceeds to say, "Boss, Side pe chalao na, Bike ko thoda rasta dena mangta hai" 

I just rolled up my window and began laughing at that point because this was the best argument for a bike lane in India.

[NP] A stupid culture I have noticed in college...is this dangerous?
body:
Okay I am pretty much aware about hookups and have done that in the past (Delhi se hun samaj lo) (also realised it's not my thing) but in college I have witnessed something which is making me lose faith in humanity. One of my close friends went through this and boy did she become a wreck for a while. 

Like a guy and girl become fast friends and then date... 'Seriously' for like 2 weeks and then suddenly one or both of them lose interest. Then total drama occurs because they know each other's personal details. Then they start psychoanalysis and make up bullshit excuses (iT wAS oNlY lIkInG, nOt tRuE lOvE)(who is even looking for true love are we in a TV show?!).   

Like if y'all wanna fuck just hookup yikes. I just don't get it. Am I missing something? Because last time I had a girlfriend I kept that thing going for 2 years. I like to be comfortable with them for a long time before I develop any romantic feelings.  

Also, when it comes to sex I don't get how you can do it with total strangers or people who'd leave you within a few days. That shit's nasty.  

You know what now that I think about how did I never experienced this before in Delhi.

title: TIL: About Global Country of World Peace, an attempt to create micronation inaugurated by Maharishi Mahesh Yogi. It has issued a currency called the "Raam"
body:
> In 2001, it was reported that the Global Country of World Peace had been unsuccessful in its attempts to establish a sovereign nation after contacting countries in Africa, Asia, and Latin America.[44][45] In November 2000, the GCWP made a proposal to the President of Suriname, offering $1.3 billion over three years for a 200-year lease of 3,500 acres (14 km2) of rural land plus "1 percent of the money the sovereign state's central bank puts into circulation" and the creation of 10,000 jobs.[45][46] The UNHCR reported that, in July 2001, the island nation of Tuvalu rejected, after serious consideration, a proposal from the GCWP to create a "Vatican like sovereign city-state" near the international airport in exchange for a payment of $2 million per year.[47]

> In 2002, the GCWP asked if they could set up their world headquarters on one hundred acres of land[34] on the 33-square-mile (85 km2) island of Rota which is part of the Commonwealth of the Northern Mariana Islands, a commonwealth of the United States. The GCWP offered to contribute to economic growth of the country but Rota declined, preferring to remain part of the Commonwealth.[48] In Costa Rica, officials of the GCWP "allegedly offered each family" on the Talamanca reservation, $250 per month for the right to appoint a king. A ceremony was held on the Talamanca reservation to appoint a local Indian as king of the community.[49] Perceiving the GCWP's actions as efforts to establish an independent state the Security Minister ordered the GCWP representative to leave the country.[50]

In need of urgent advice regarding career! Please help.
body:
Hi,

I am a 25 year old Indian national living in Thailand with my family. I did my undergrad in Thailand in BBA in Finance. After that I joined my family trading business of loose diamonds. After a year in the business, I joined a Thailand based diamond jewelry export company (owned by an Indian-American). 

I am working in the jewelry company for the past two years but since I am the youngest I am given odd works. I am being paid 55,000 baht per month (after tax). 

I was wondering what my options for a secure future are? I am starting to get complacent in my job - getting comfortable with my monthly salary which I do not want. If I get fired or if I want to switch my job, I do not feel I have enough skills to get an equivalent salary. 

Another issue is that the jewelry industry is shrinking. I am also not sure if I enjoy the industry as much. I have a few options in mind but opinions are needed. My options are -
1. CFA
2. MBA
3. Starting a business

I am so confused in life about my career that I hate even thinking about it. My work timings are 8:30 am to 6:30 pm (Mon-Fri). Saturdays I work 8:30 am to 12:00 pm.

I have around 10 lakh baht saved since I live with my family. But we do not have any asset - no house, car, etc.

Please help.

Edit - For MBA, I would love to go to the Western countries or India - wouldn't do it in Thailand. I do not mind shifting back to India if required. My family is not very well off. My parents do not have a lot of money save for their retirement.

title: Asking for some useful advice before reapplying for schengen Visa
body:
Randians, I am planning to visit Schengen area Solo in late October and early November for about 2 weeks. I am planning to visit **Barcelona , Amsterdam , Paris and Brussels(2-3 days every place)**. Actually the issue is i tried to do the same sort of trip solo at the very same time (coincidence, isn't it?) **via Germany about 2 years back** when i was a student and had sufficient funds (transferred enough money from my dad's account to mine about 6-7 months before applying) and proper paperwork, **the embassy still rejected my tourist visa with only reason that my application is failing to have the needed justification for your purpose and conditions of the planned travel and stay(i think i should have shown dad's statement instead of mine(silly me), a friend did this last year when he went to UK)**.

*I had paid lodging and everything in Berlin through AirBnB . I appealed against refusal but they still didn't approve my visa.* 

After that I travelled in South East Asia for 2 weeks about a year ago so that my passport has at least one stamp. Now I am employed(first job) since last 7 months(I don't have an ITR document , i joined in the last month of FY). 

**I am planning to apply through Spain(9% rejection rate) or Nederlands also high rejection rate.** 

Now guys i need your **help and tips so that i could avoid Visa refusal again**. Also  I was also thinking of **applying through Estonia (2% rejection rate)  and then move from there to Barcelona or Amsterdam** or whatever nearest .Also this time when i am employed ,should i get a letter written up by my parents or show their bank statements instead of mine? I hope you guys could help me out and guide me so that i can make a kickass application.  Thanks!

title: Is Apple generally this horrible or did I get plain unlucky?
body:
Last year, I bought my wife a 2017 Macbook Air as a birthday gift. It worked great, looked great and was everything she wanted in a laptop

So this year, I bit the bullet and bought myself a Macbook Air as well, the one with the new keyboard. This was in March

Fast forward to 4 months later and my new Macbook Air has a keyboard with a spacebar that doesn't work (which basically means that the laptop is useless). Apple replaces it (in 2 fucking weeks) and acknowledges that it's an ongoing issue with all Macbooks with the new butterfly switches. They also tell me that it will happen again so be prepared for it (in other words: buy our extended warranty).

Around the same time, my wife's Macbook Air starts freezing randomly. Unfortunately, her laptop's warranty expired while we were traveling and we couldn't buy the extended warranty in time (which costs nearly 33% of the laptop's cost, btw). 

Apple said that the laptop's logic board is screwed and will cost 40k to replace. I bought the laptop itself for 57k.

In 1 year, I've spent over 1.5L on Macbooks and both of these have been lemons. Meanwhile, my 6 year old Lenovo is still going strong, as is my other 3 year Lenovo

Is this the general Apple experience?

Intolerant India , you get abused for following the rules
body:
Have you ever seen people breaking the queue to get a rail ticket? I think this an every day scene at the counter. I am a daily passenger and I had to renew my quarterly pass , thus I arrived at station 40 mins prior to my train departure. I issue a first class pass but as there were only 20 people I joined the queue and waited for my turn. A lady came rushing and went straight to the window , at this moment 10-12 mins must have passed and queue was growing as 2 major trains depart and morning rush hour had begun.
I requested her to join the line and got a reply “train is about to come , will miss it” . I said everyone waiting is to board the same train and I informed the ticket guy to not issue a ticket to her. At last she had to leave and she shouted the words “Idiot bastard” and rushed away shedding her pride. Am I the idiot/bastard in this case ? Why do people get offended when they are being simply told to explain simple rules every citizen has to follow. I know this is very common but I am more offended coz of her retaliation. Also I don’t know why 80% of us don’t speak up against these literate fools who think they have the right to oppress other fellow citizens. I could have abused her right away but didn’t think it was required .
(I have seen people getting butt kicked for this in Mumbai and many metros just to strengthen my argument)

title: How to get rid of an annoying relative/family member who has overstayed his welcome?
body:
So, y'all know how close family members are in Indian (and probably most Asian) societies. But we have this family member, let's call him 'K', who has been living at our house for the last 3 days? K has visited us quite a few times and stayed for a night or two in the past. The reason he's staying at our place is cause he got transferred to the city we live in (due to his own stupidity, I must add). 

Now, my family is going through a tough time, so we have problems of our own, while this motherfucker shows up everyday, sits in front of the AC from 4PM to 9AM (after he's back and until before he leaves for work), lying there like a fuckin' sunbathing crocodile. Nobody at our home uses the AC for more than 4-5 hours, and that's usually from 10PM to 2-3AM (when Mom or Dad wake up and turn it off). While my family is financial stable, I hate this freeloader dude comin' in, 3-4 days a week, using up more shampoo than the 3 of us combined, eating like a starving elephant, consuming electricity like there is no tomorrow....and more, but you get the point.

Oh, and did I mention that K's wife back home is sick? And he's sending his kid homework answers on freaking WhatsApp. Also, this dude can easily take like 30 days off no problem (cause of a fairly high 'post' in a government job). God, so frustrating to handle him cause he also wants to poke his nose in all our family matters.

TL;DR: Annoying family member staying at our place, freeloading shit, while his wife is sick and kid needs help with homework and he can actually take a leave while he works on transferring back to his city.

Any solutions?

title: How can I converse in English properly?
body:
I am your average middle class guy who lived in a middle class society, interacted with middle class people and studied in an English medium school where majority of the time we talked in Hindi itself.

When it comes to writing in English, I guess, I am good enough but the real problem comes when it comes to speaking. Most of the time when I talk in English, like in an interview or something, I first think in Hindi and then translate whatever I want to speak which leads to a lot of 'ummm...ahhh, you know' moments which is awkward. It is not like I can't speak properly but I just do not feel comfortable speaking in English.

I am finally placed and will be joining the corporate world in Jul 2020. I wanted to know about ways in which I can improve my English speaking skills.

How developed are Indian cities compared to foreign cities in their league economy wise?
body:
There is far less data about regional GDP/PPP/GNI per capita but still I managed to find some cities for comparison: 

India(Delhi)-$20,139 PPP per capita(2018)

China(Chongqing)-$18,783 PPP per capita(2018)

Vietnam(Ho Chi Minh City)-$21,167 PPP per capita(2018)

Philippines(Manila)-$25,843 PPP per capita(2017)

What do people who have been to Delhi and one of the three other cities think about the comparison? Feel free to add in more foreign cities if you can post PPP per capita of that city which should be around $20,000. 

Also Chongqing is different as nearly half of its population is rural, it is the most rural Chinese municipality, but it's the closest to $20,000 among major Chinese cities so I included it anyway, by some metrics these four cities are the most populous cities in their respective countries too.

M(22) first solo travel to India - Help appreciated :)
body:
Hello r/India,

Hope this is the right place to post this :)

I'm about to go on my first solo trip from end of October to mid-December in India.   
The trip will be around 6-7 weeks and I have little idea on where to start where to end and what to see.

I do have some ideas which I want to mention here, but please feel free to comment!   
1. The route in my head is starting in the north (probably New Delhi) and then going south along the west coast - this was based on a quick research of climatic factors.  
2. I really want to do a 10-day Vipassana course during my travels (not sure where)   
3. I want to include some hiking/nature - I was thinking more of a rainforest kind of experience than the Himalaya  
4. I want to see Goa (please let me know, where the good spots are and whether you think it is worth it)   
5. If possible I'd love to learn how to surf, so if that is possible, let me know where the best surfing spots are  


So that is pretty much all I have so far.   
Accommodation-wise I will most likely sleep in hostels.   


Please let me know your thoughts on the trip.   
What are the absolute hot spots in India? What can't be missed?   
What are some amazing hiking routes? Which guides can you recommend?   
Other travel tips are very much appreciated!  


Thank you so much :)

Kashmiri here, how are you as a mainland Indian viewing the revelations from Kashmir? Has your perception about Kashmir changed by seeing the stark difference between govt propaganda and western media coverage?
body:
Do you now believe that whatever you were told about Kashmir for last so many years was factually incorrect? How do you view all this and Indian state as general now?

​

p.s. Asking this here on Reddit because elsewhere it's just noise.

​

Edit: 

Thank you all for your inputs, I'll leave with following few points

1. Kashmir dispute is older than Ind and Pakistan, repression is 5 centuries old. 
2. Accession happened without consent of public and that is why plebiscite was promised- to respect public opinion. And also it is the only peaceful agreement that has consent of both the nations and Kashmiris. 
3. Autonomy of Kashmir was eroded since '57. Pro-Indian politicians were shown their place time and again throughout the political timeline of Kashmir. You can Google that it is nothing new this time.
4. What happened in 1990s- Pandit exodus is a blot on our Kashmiri society, it was partly orchestrated by govt of the day, partly induced through threat and intimidation by KMs, KMs to an extent failed them. However, another non-muslim minority-Sikhs never left, ask yourself, why? 
5. Kashmiri Sikhs have always been there when KMs needed them, always. We are forever indebted to them. 
6. The thing with Kashmir is that Kashmiri voices get lost between the din of propaganda on both the sides. We are like you, we have families and friends, we don't want to lose our loved ones to violence. You people get agitated when you Uber is late imagine our state, it has been 30 days and we have no news from our home.
7. I wonder how sub-continent would have been had there been no Kashmir issue since both India and Pak are spending millions on Kashmir that money would have ushered new development in our part of the world. As Orwell said, the war is never meant to be won it is only meant to be continuous. 
8. Kashmir is deemed to be economically backward due to joblessness which we are to a fair extent and yet we are the sole reason why Billion dollar arms industries exist- Chutzpah of its own kind.
9. Lastly, I apologize to anyone who might be hurt by my disagreement. I surely could have disagreed in a better way.
